elements.sent-2411-001: This paper continues on the program of developing a relativistic quantum information theory in terms of unequal-time correlation functions in quantum field theory (QFT) [C.. Anastopoulos, B.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here, we focus on the definition of quantum resources from the irreducibly quantum behavior contained in the correlation functions of a QFT.
elements.sent-2411-005: We explain how set-ups with $N$ particle detectors probe the information in the high order field correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our main object is the associated hierarchy of probability densities of $N$-detector events.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that classical probabilistic hierarchies are subject to two conditions: Kolmogorov additivity and measurement independence.
elements.sent-2411-008: QFT violates those conditions, and the degree of violation enables us to define novel quantum resources.
elements.sent-2411-009: We give specific examples in set-ups where the main observables are the times of particle detection events.
elements.sent-2411-010: The new resources capture instances of irreducibly quantum behavior that differs from the quantum behavior encapsulated in Bell inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-011: An interesting byproduct of our analysis is a relativistic state reduction rule for particles detected through scattering.
elements.sent-2411-012: Understanding the relation of quantum information theory (QIT) to relativity is a major and multi-faceted task.
elements.sent-2411-013: On one hand, it involves the application of quantum information concepts to fundamental problems of relativistic physics, as they appear in diverse research fields, including particle physics, cosmology, quantum gravity, and quantum optics.
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, it requires the incorporation of the fundamental principles of relativity, namely, causality and covariance, into the foundations of QIT.
elements.sent-2411-015: This will create novel opportunities for both theory and technological applications.
elements.sent-2411-016: To achieve those ends, the grounding of quantum information concepts on quantum field theory (QFT) is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-017: QFT is the only known theory that combines quantum theory and special relativistic dynamics consistently, and in agreement with experiments.
elements.sent-2411-018: Its domain of validity extends along the full range of physics, from quarks to condensed matter, to black holes, and the early universe.
elements.sent-2411-019: So far, the mainstream QIT paradigms have been developed in the context of non-relativistic quantum mechanics, which is a small corner of full QFT.
elements.sent-2411-020: Certainly, a transfer of non-relativistic concepts to relativistic setups is possible, and, indeed, fruitful.
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, some aspects of entanglement are commonly discussed in QFT, especially in relation to black hole information and holography---see, for example, \cite{CaCa06, Witten .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, entanglement in QFT does not have the informational interpretation that it has in non-relativistic quantum information theory.
elements.sent-2411-023: In the latter context, entanglement is identified as a quantum resource associated to the Local Operation and Classical Communication (LOCC) protocol \cite{LOCC .}
elements.sent-2411-024: There is no relativistic analogue for LOCC, mainly due to our limited knowledge about admissible quantum operations in QFT, especially in relation to relativistic locality and causality.
elements.sent-2411-025: Hence, a first-principles informational interpretation of entanglement in QFT, in terms of information obtained from measurements is missing.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, relativistic covariance suggests the need of a unified treatment of spacelike quantum correlations (as in Bell non-separability \cite{Bell ) and temporal ones (as in the violation of the Leggett-Garg inequalities \cite{LeGa ).}}
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper is part of a larger program to develop a consistent relativistic QIT from first principles \cite{AnSav22, AHS23 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: This program emphasizes the need of a consistent and practicable measurement theory for relativistic QFT.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is a long-standing problem in the foundations of QFT.
elements.sent-2411-030: A major part of the problem is the lack of a consistent relativistic rule for state update after measurements.
elements.sent-2411-031: A naive transfer of the rule for quantum state reduction used in non-relativistic quantum theory to a relativistic setup consists with relativistic causality ---see \cite{PeTe, AnSav22, AHS23, MD and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Modern QFT has sidestepped such problems by focusing on scattering experiments that can be treated via S-matrix theory---see Refs.. \cite{Blum,MD .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The latter treats scattering as a process with a single measurement event in the asymptotic future, so the state-update rule is not used.
elements.sent-2411-034: However, a state-update rule is needed to construct joint probabilities for multiple measurement events, and no probabilistic theory can work in its absence.
elements.sent-2411-035: It is required in the analysis of Bell-type experiments, experiments involving temporal quantum correlations, and for post-selected measurements.
elements.sent-2411-036: In particular, quantum optics requires joint probabilities, to describe photon bunching and anti-bunching.
elements.sent-2411-037: To this end, photo-detection theories have been developed, the most prominent of which is that of Glauber \cite{Glauber1, Glauber2 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Glauber's theory was arguably the first QFT measurement theory, and it proved remarkably successful.
elements.sent-2411-040: It works only for photons, and it involves approximations that may not be consistent with relativistic causality.
elements.sent-2411-041: Early attempts to describe QFT measurements include the formal treatment of Hellwig and Kraus \cite{HK , the development of detector models by Unruh and DeWitt \cite{Unruh76, Dewitt , and Sorkin's important argument about the conflict between ideal measurements and QFT causality \cite{Sorkin .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Recent years have witnessed renewed interest on the topic; see, for example, Refs.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this work, we describe QFT measurements using the Quantum Temporal Probabilities method (QTP) \cite{QTP1, QTP2, QTP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The original motivation of QTP was to provide a general framework for temporally extended quantum observables \cite{AnSav06 , hence the name.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The key idea in QTP is to distinguish between the time parameter of Schr\"odinger's equation from the time variable associated to particle detection \cite{Sav99, Sav10 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The latter is then treated as a macroscopic variable associated to the detector degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-047: A major difference of QTP from other approaches to QFT measurements is that it describes measurements within the decoherent histories approach to quantum mechanics \cite{Gri, Omn1, Omn2, GeHa1, GeHa2, hartlelo , rather than trying to connect with von Neumann's measurement theory \cite{vN, Busch .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: This means that QTP focusses on the emergent classicality in the measurement apparatus.
elements.sent-2411-049: Observables correspond to coarse-grained histories of the apparatus' degrees of freedom that satisfy a decoherence condition.
elements.sent-2411-050: QTP emphasizes that every measurement event is localized in spacetime, and that the time of its occurrence is a random variable.
elements.sent-2411-051: This contrasts the von Neumann description of quantum measurements, in which the time of a measurement event is {\em a priori fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The QTP treatment is closer to experimental practice.
elements.sent-2411-053: Actual particle detectors are fixed in space and the timing of their recordings varies probabilistically.
elements.sent-2411-054: In QTP, the random variables for each detection event are of the form $(x, q)$, where $x$ is the spacetime point of detection, and $q$ refers collectively to all other observables (particle momentum, angular momentum, and so on).
elements.sent-2411-055: Hence, QTP provides probabilities densities of the form \bey P_n(x_1, q_1; x_2, q_2; \ldots ; x_n, q_n), \eey associated to set-ups with $n$ separate detectors.
elements.sent-2411-056: The probability densities (\ref{pnnn ) are linear functionals of unequal time correlation of the fields with $2n$ entries.}
elements.sent-2411-057: This is particularly important, because such correlation functions are a staple of QFT, and there exists a powerful arsenal of techniques for their calculation.
elements.sent-2411-058: The specific correlation functions relevant to QTP are not the usual ones of S-matrix theory (in-out formalism), but they appear in the Closed-Time-Path (CTP) (Schwinger-Keldysh or `in-in') formalism \cite{ctp1, ctp2, ctp3, ctp4, ctp5 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Unlike $S$-matrix theory, the CTP formalism allows for the evaluation of real-time---rather than asymptotic---probabilities for the quantum fields.
elements.sent-2411-060: It has found many applications in nuclear-particle physics, early Universe cosmology, and condensed matter physics \cite{CH08, Berges, coma1, coma2 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The connection between the two formalisms allows us to combine the tools of quantum measurement theory and QFT, that is, the language of Positive-Operator-Valued measures (POVMs) and effects, with the manifestly covariant language of path integrals.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Quantum information from the correlation hierarchy
elements.sent-2411-062: A crucial fact about QFTs is that all information is encoded into hierarchies of unequal-time correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, Wightman's reconstruction theorem \cite{Wightman1, WiSt allows one to construct the full QFT and the ground state from the properties of a theory's Wightman functions, that is, vacuum expectation values of products of field operators.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The Lehmann-Symanzik-Zimmermann construction enables the calculation of all elements of the S-matrix from the hierarchy of time-ordered correlation functions \cite{LSZ .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In the general case, all real-time properties of a QFT can be constructed from the CTP hierarchy of unequal-time field correlation functions \cite{cddn, DCH \bey G^{m, n (x_1, \ldots, x_n; x_1', \ldots x'_m) = Tr \left[{\cal T ^*[\hat{\phi (x_n) \ldots \hat{\phi (x_1)]\hat{\rho _0{\cal T [\hat{\phi (x_1) \ldots \hat{\phi (x_n)] \right], \eey where $\hat{\phi}$ is an arbitrary field, $\hat{\rho _0$ is the initial state of the field, ${\cal T}$ stands for time-ordering and ${\cal T ^*$ for reverse time ordering.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: This means that the hierarchy of the unequal-time correlation functions contains all information about the QFT, including Bell-type and dynamical quantum correlations.
elements.sent-2411-067: The starting point of our analysis is that this information can be extracted through QTP-type measurements.
elements.sent-2411-068: For each type of detector that records events of the form $(x, q)$, there exists a hierarchy of probability densities (\ref{pnnn ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: Each member of the hierarchy corresponds to a measurement set-up with $n$ detectors of this type, and it extracts information corresponding to the correlation function $G^{n,n}$ of the CTP hierarchy---see Fig. \ref{hierar .}

elements.sent-2411-070: In this paper, we identify the irreducibly quantum characteristics in QTP probability hierarchies, and, consequently, of the associated CTP field correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-071: The key observation is that classical probabilistic hierarchies are subject to constraints, due to compatibility conditions between the different levels of the hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-072: We show that these constraints are violated in quantum theory.
elements.sent-2411-073: Then, the size of this violation, as quantified by a norm, defines a quantum resource associated to the probabilistic hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-074: We identify two types of classicality conditions in probabilistic hierarchies, {\em Kolmogorov additivity and {\em measurement independence .. 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: A probabilistic hierarchy can be obtained from a single stochastic probability measure, if it satisfies Kolmogorov additivity.
elements.sent-2411-076: This is a compatibility condition between any level of the hierarchy and its lower ones.
elements.sent-2411-077: Quantum theory can violate Kolmogorov additivity, a feature that has also been identified in non-relativistic set-ups \cite{Ana06, BrKo, ClKo , and it is closely related to the violation of the Leggett-Garg inequalities \cite{Halli1, Halli2 .. 2.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: A hierarchy that satisfies Kolmogorov additivity is described by a stochastic process, but this process is not necessarily local.
elements.sent-2411-079: The appropriate locality condition is measurement independence \cite{MI1, MI2, MI3 , which roughly asserts that the physical quantity that is being measured in a detector is defined independently of any other quantity that is being measured in the same set-up.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In non-relativistic physics, measurement independence is a prerequisite for the derivation of Bell's inequality.
elements.sent-2411-081: The two conditions above constrain probability densities from different levels in the probabilistic hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-082: This contrasts Bell-type inequalities \cite{Bell that--in the present context--require the comparison of probability densities from {\em different probabilistic hierarchies.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: For example, the derivation of the Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt (CHSH) inequality \cite{CHSH considers four different pairs of detectors, and each pair would have to be embedded at the level $n = 2$ of a different probabilistic hierarchy.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this paper, we analyze the implications of the classicality constraints above, and then we define the quantum resources defined by their violation.
elements.sent-2411-085: For concreteness, we evaluate the latter for the simplest probabilistic hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-086: This consists of probability densities of the form (\ref{pnnn ), in which the only recorded observable is the spacetime point of the detection event.}

elements.sent-2411-338: The main result of this paper is the analysis of quantum resources in QFT.
elements.sent-2411-339: We showed that the QTP description of measurements enables the construction of probabilistic hierarchies that probe the information content in the QFT hierarchy of unequal-time correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-340: In this setting, we analyzed quantum resources, in particular ones that refer to the compatibility between different levels of the probabilistic hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-341: We identified two types of irreducibly quantum behavior.
elements.sent-2411-342: The first is associated with the failure of Kolmogorov additivity, and the second with the failure of measurement independence.
elements.sent-2411-343: Then, we considered specific examples of those resources, by focusing on measurements in which the main random variable is the detection times of relativistic particles.
elements.sent-2411-344: A by-product of this analysis was the derivation of a reduction rule for relativistic particles that are recorded---through scattering---at a spacetime point $x$.
elements.sent-2411-345: This paper is guided by the belief that the unequal time correlation functions are the essential physical content of QFT.
elements.sent-2411-346: This implies that any relativistic approach to quantum information must focus on the properties of the hierarchy of such correlation functions, and not on the properties of the quantum state as in non-relativistic physics.
elements.sent-2411-347: The correlation functions contain information that pertain both to the initial (and occasionally final) conditions of a QFT and to dynamics, and in a relativistic setting it is impossible to separate them\footnote{There are mathematical reasons for this.}
elements.sent-2411-348: For example, to select one out of the infinite representations of the canonical (anti)commutation relations for a quantum field, one requires that an operator corresponding to the classical field Hamiltonian is defined on the relevant Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-349: This is particularly relevant for the discussion of information loss in black-hole evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-350: In previous work, we have shown that multi-detector measurements (carried out at finite times) reveal that the Hawking radiation is not thermal or close to thermal \cite{AnSav19, AnSav19b .}
elements.sent-2411-351: Only the probabilities associated to single-event measurements are thermal.
elements.sent-2411-352: In this paper, we quantified quantum behavior using simple $L^1$ and supremum norms for the violation of classicality conditions.
elements.sent-2411-353: Entropic measures might be more appropriate for applications to non-equilibrium QFT.
elements.sent-2411-354: We recall that the level $n=1$ of the probabilistic hierarchy is the level at which the Boltzmann equation---and the thermodynamic entropy---is defined \cite{CH08 .}
elements.sent-2411-355: A relation between thermodynamic entropy and entropic measures of quantum correlations from the higher field correlations could be important for understanding the origins of macroscopic irreversibility.
elements.sent-2411-356: Our longer term aim is to define quantum resources with reference solely to the hierarchy of correlation functions, and not in relation to specific measurement set-ups, as in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-357: This would provide a unified treatment of quantum resources in QFT that would bring together Kolmogorov non-additivity, violation of measurement independence (as defined here), and Bell-type non-locality.
elements.sent-2411-358: To this end, it is necessary to have a general characterization of all measurements admissible in QFT, i.e., to identify all POVMs that are compatible with relativistic causality.
elements.sent-2411-359: At the moment, this seems a formidable task.
elements.sent-2411-360: We expect that the irreducible quantum behavior identified in this paper is experimentally accessible.
elements.sent-2411-361: For example, we noticed that the violation of the inequality (\ref{p221 ) is already established in anti-bunching experiments for photons.}
elements.sent-2411-362: In a future publication, we will analyse how the detection-time correlations of Sec.
elements.sent-2411-363: The violation of Kolmogorov additivity is more intricate, but it may be possible to employ variations of setups that test macrorealism through the violation of the Leggett-Garg inequality \cite{LeGare .}
elements.sent-2411-364: Finally, we note that in our analysis, the state-reduction rule arises as a secondary consequence of the probability rule for multi-detector measurements.
elements.sent-2411-365: It is certainly not a physical process, and this is why it may carry information from subsequent measurements, when we condition our statistical ensemble upon detection.
elements.sent-2411-366: This treatment of reduction is in accordance with ideas first expressed by Wigner \cite{WH65 , and made explicit in the decoherent histories program.}
elements.sent-2411-367: Nonetheless, our methodology and the specific expressions for reduction derived here may be useful to the program of dynamical reduction \cite{GhBa , since these expressions are derived from a fully-fledged relativistic quantum theory.}
